topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The thin, cream-colored paper felt slick under my fingertips as I unfolded the envelope. Inside, nestled between glossy brochures, was a single essay. My stomach tightened immediately, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. It was my own story, the rambling, painfully earnest piece I’d poured my soul into during the frantic final weeks of senior year. But it wasn't mine. The header read: "Example Submission: Narrative Voice Showcase." My breath hitched. I traced the perfectly formed, overly polished sentences with a trembling finger. Every carefully crafted metaphor felt like a mockery. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound a sharp, unpleasant rustle in the quiet dorm room. I stood up abruptly, needing space, needing to scrub the feeling off my skin.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my jaw tight enough to ache. It was an online portal, a place I’d expected to be filled with hopeful futures, but instead, I found it: my personal narrative, meticulously edited, presented as a model for aspiring applicants. The font was too elegant, the anecdotes too neat, the emotional arc too predictable. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. "This isn't me," I whispered to the empty room, the sound thin and reedy. My hands began to shake uncontrollably, and I shoved the laptop away as if it were suddenly hot, refusing to look at the pristine prose any longer.

[story 3]

When the admissions office emailed the link, I almost didn't click. My heart hammered against my sternum, a frantic, uneven rhythm. But curiosity, or perhaps a morbid compulsion, pulled me forward. The essay was flawless. It spoke of resilience and profound introspection with a fluency I couldn't muster, a polished veneer over what I felt was messy and real. Reading it felt like watching a stranger perform a flawless imitation of my own struggles. I closed my eyes, pressing the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block the words from entering my mind. When I finally opened them, the paper seemed tainted, the very ink mocking the vulnerability I’d tried so hard to share.
